content:
A few points here :
1. csv conversion should be done by the studio : this includes choosing a proper character set, ensuring the line breaks are just raw `\n` symbols and not actual line breaks  (impossible in .csv). *This ensures nothing can break regarding the conversions & formatting*
2. the files should be uploaded in full to the repo, and ideally we should work on those. They may be split-up thematically but if we just have an index of what regions of the files have what, it's good enough. This means that we can finally use git properly : no more bits of files named after us that can't merge properly.

Git already signs your commits with your account name, the date at which it's made and allows you to attach a lil' message to it. Just write in that message what your commit is about in terms of action, scope, and then explain in human-readable terms what you've done (see https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/#summary for an example of *a* norm widely used is done)

This means that all the work you wanna do with your merging of files signed by people is completely redundant with git itself, and serves no real purpose.
Splitting the files into thematic chunks would still work, and as long as that's not a hassle on BIAV's part I'd be down for it, though limiting the friction for what needs to happen for our work to get shipped would be nice.

embeds.title: Conventional Commits
embeds.description: A specification for adding human and machine readable meaning to commit messages

content:
for example, this is the amount of info a commit contains **[see image]**:
- a unique hash identifying it (*only the first 7 characters of it are shown here*)
- an author (+ the email address or another identifier)
- a date
- a message (here, saying that nothing of importance was made, and that it's inside a specific code region)
- the actual content, represented here with fancy colors to highlight what's removed and what's added, from what file(s) and at what position(s)

That's all we need to process everything very fast, assuming everyone would write the commit messages with enough info.

Also, having the files and modifying them means it's easy to just accommodate for other people working on the same files.
Example :
- you're working on rewording Aigis' cards
- someone else is working on replacing all the verbs used for shields & aliemus with the "right ones". This someone pushes his changes first.

You've just finished your changes, how do you make sure you can send them without breaking anything of the other person's work ?

Well, if you `git rebase` (or just click the "sync fork" button), git will tell you where there is conflict between the modifications on your side & the current state of the repo, and ask you to find a way to resolve it.
Meaning you can see where you both touched things, what you both did, and then resolve the conflict.
For example, you could just reapply all the changes you made to the version with the new verbs, or decide that with those verbs you should touch up a bit of what you've done, etc.
And then you can send in your work.

No more of Ace/BIAV having to check if any of the 35 files that touch the english card text may have some regions in common that might have different modifications and look up/guess what the authors intended for the modification to be in order to deduce what the ultimate version is (*here, the authors would always send in "just their changes" and all conflicts would be resolved by the authors themselves*)

Its name probably originated from this
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tohu_wa-bohu
in the terminology chart the game seems to abbreviate it as tohuwabohu… while I prefer to remain as original with the hyphen

embeds.title: Tohu wa-bohu
embeds.description:
Tohu wa-bohu or Tohu va-Vohu (Biblical Hebrew: תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ‎ ṯōhū wāḇōhū) is a Biblical Hebrew phrase found in the  Genesis creation narrative (Genesis 1:2), which describes the condition of the earth ('aretz) immediately before the creation of light in Genesis 1:3.
Numerous interpretations of this

content: oh my... thanks a lot!

I feel this works great for most projects, but we're working with spreadsheets, and it seems to me like it'd be difficult for Git to handle columns. I'm aware of GitXL, but have no idea how to use it or what it does exactly. 

Here's the solution I've come up with:
A well-organized public repo anyone can push to *(relying, of course, on people following relatively simple instructions and not vandalizing, though I would have a backup)*, with each chunk having a ledger file. All files would be in .xlsx format. When someone wants to work on a file, they sign their name in the ledger under the language they're working on. When they're done, they upload their file into that folder, and when I have time, I compile their work into the base file and open up that space in the ledger by writing down their contributions.

Every solution has its pros and cons. I see two issues with this solution. First, if we get new source sheets, I'd have to update all of my repo's files with the new sheets. I have no good solution for this one aside from keeping my files in as large of chunks as reasonably manageable, so that the manual work on my end is lesser, but I'm 100% fine with that being my job if it helps everyone else out a little.

Second, if someone wants to do minor corrections on a mass scale to multiple different things, they would have to download, sign, and upload multiple files.
My solutions to this are:
1. No one *has* to use this repo. They can always default back to the main BIAV repo and do the work in whatever format they want there. When pull requests get confirmed there, **I'll** do the coordination work of updating all of my repo's files with that change. Again, I really don't mind doing that. Gonna have to do it with the current batch of compiled work to get my repo up to date with the BIAV repo anyway, so I'll learn some shortcuts along the way.
2. Again, keep my organized chunks as large but concise as possible, especially in regards to gameplay, so that chunks that have similar terminology are grouped together. I've been pretty good at that so far, or so I hope.

My goal here is to minimalize the amount of potential risk that could arise from anyone who wants to do volunteer work being confused about any of this.

> I feel this works great for most projects, but we're working with spreadsheets
Well that's the things, we aren't really.
The text in the sheet presents references to ***.csv*** files
(ex : `Skill_4122_Desc_0,aMqS,j技能表_Skill_A.csv,1152,Skill,Desc,凯刻斯,获得 <Block:[Block:Arg1]> 点护盾。凯刻斯获得 <Energy:[Energy:Arg2]> 点狂气。,Obtain <Block:[Block:Arg1]> Shield. Caecus obtains <Energy:[Energy:Arg2]> Aliemus.` contains `j技能表_Skill_A.csv`), and generally Ace has been hinting at the fact that the google sheet/spreadsheet nature of the source files is just something made for us.

We're dealing with .csv files, and conversions using awful tools like microsoft excel are irrelevant to the data.
Also, ***.xlsx*** is ass as a format, if we're to use another thing than csv, at least go for ***.ods*** and using libreOffice (open, free and available on basically every OS).


Handling columns is as simple as pre-filling them with nothing (adding commas at the end of every line until there are as many as needed, though that's where having a language file system like i18n for the webapps does would be better).

As for the new content or anything BIAV pushes, well they can just slap it on the repo.
They can literally export the ***.csv*** file from whatever they use and put it in place of the old one, and git will magically calculate the difference if you're curious about it, but most importantly, you'll be able to work on top of those new changes instantly.

I can supply the organized files as UTF-8 .csv if you think that's better for everyone than .xlsx. I've been using .xlsx because it can handle a lot more organizational features like multiple sheets in one workbook, colored columns, merged cells etc. and that's *(or so I've been told)* been really helpful for folks trying to work with these files. If .ods has all of these features as well, and if Excel can properly handle that conversion, I can do that, but I'd prefer to do as little converting as possible to minimize the risk of something going awry. 

Yes we're dealing with .csv files, but they were presented to us as an .xlsx, so I've been sticking with that.
Also, I'm not sure if it's super relevant to the ultimate aim of the discussion but like... I assumed that "spreadsheet" meant "a file in which data is separated by rows and columns" and with that definition I don't understand how .csv files "aren't really" spreadsheets.

As for the explanations of how Git works, I super appreciate knowing more about it, but I'm not sure how to apply this information into a solution that works better than my proposal. 😅 What do you propose as an organizational structure that would work better? I'll say my evaluation of potential solutions is biased by my preference that I do the "technical work" instead of leaving that to individual volunteers' understanding of Git.
here's the thing, to my understanding, 100% of the work you're planning to do is redundant with automated work done in milliseconds at the press of a button in a neatly organised repo
let's talk about how to organize that repo then~
would a voice call be better than text?

also, I'd say .csv files are spreadsheets that are aware that they're actually text documents.

Basically they're the simplest representation of things in cells, and the file itself contains the data, whereas what's commonly referred to as spreadsheets  stores its data in a complex structure due to the incredible amount of other information that it needs aside from the content of a cell

kind of like markdown & html if you will, markdown is legible, and you can choose to view the "result" of the formatting, whereas html can very easily be incomprehensible in its 

example: can you guess what this is ?
Well, that's a sentence with math symbols

Much like that, if you looked at the content of a .xlsl file itself (which is what git does), you'd have a lot of trouble determining what is what, where is what, and what the fuck is the difference with the previous version of that file.
Git is a tool that works on text files, and you do not benefit (much) from using it on other things than that.
(don't get me wrong it's super cool to have my images in a repo that can change depending on the version or branch, but the main interest of git is all the stuff that comes with it being able to compare revisions of a file and present that info to you the user, in order to help organize your work with the other people touching roughly the same stuff)

I'd be down for a call tomorrow evening fr time , since it's 11PM here and I work.
Would sometime between <t:1757952000> & <t:1757966400> be fine for you ?
I also work on weekdays 😅 so maybe call isn't gonna work out cus of timezones lol

while I totally agree with Goz's point of view in that Git has existing tools to do all of this with far less work, the practical issue imo is that, well, first I'm unsure if BIAV/Ace is actually taking advantage of any of those features, and second, there is a technical barrier of entry and a learning curve with plenty of room for mistakes in the process

we can already see from some of the other languages that a lot of the translators are just working in groups rather than individually committing changes and there is some level of technical confusion going on
due to that I do think there is a spot for a coordinator to serve as a middleman for providing some level of user-friendliness to lower the barrier of entry

Depending on a someone rather than just having resources/people that can help you decide what button to click seems like an unnecessary point of failure.

I'm not expecting people to fully understand git, I'm down to explain as much as possible as many times as needed for a pleasant experience, but if people can click a magic button that does exactly what they need, why have them depend on someone to press it for them instead of teaching them how to find that button ?
Github is pretty ok in terms of features even if you're fully limiting yourself to the website itself (and not the service), so how about we help the volunteers get up to speed with the  tasks & "problems" they're most likely to encounter ?

That's a huge load of work off the hands of a "coordinator" for a minor load of teachings & making tutorials.

I'm not gonna teach you to unfuck your repo by reading the reflog¹, but just explaning the base concepts & how to rebase on top of a moving main branch to "follow the changes" made by others seems like it'd be very approachable for everyone.

-# ¹ it's most likely not gonna be fucked up any way

> Depending on a someone rather than just having resources/people that can help you decide what button to click seems like an unnecessary point of failure.
fully agree with this, and this was also my original belief. the main reason I've somewhat turned around in thought is seeing much less participation than I expected, and I'm unsure if it's simply a matter of people signing up but not actually wanting to do a work, an issue of time/effort, or if it's a technical barrier to entry preventing people from getting involved to begin with

in theory it's pretty nice to say "hey just check the documentation if you want to do x", but from a practical real-world perspective, a lot of people are probably just going to tune out or give up the moment anything more complicated than "hey you can just doubleclick on the shiny icon and type in some words when they show up on your screen" is required

like git is super useful and the stuff it can do is great, but also learning the features/usage of it and incorporating that into your workflow is...honestly a lot harder than it might seem for a non-dev user whose specialty is writing out text rather than wrangling computers
like an english major probably isn't going to pick up git as easily as a comp sci major, but the english major is probably going to be much better at the actual work we're looking for

for me at this point I'd just like to see more people participating, so if the tradeoff there is having someone who just goes "hey guys I made a google sheets you can open in your browser and type in some text and not have to worry about spending even a second thinking about complicated technology stuff" then I'd still think it's worth it

(which to be fair, from the sounds of it are pretty much what the german and russian guys are already doing? not too sure since I don't have access to those channels so I don't actually really even know how active the non-english communities are)

Well, here it's not that, it's "*hey guys I made another repo with a different unique set of rules where you can open files in a format that they aren't meant for and modify the mand them send them with only 90% of the usual hassle*"
yeah true, in this case I'm not really sure about having a separate repo, I think that's not really the best idea

at best I'd say it should be a google drive or sheets or something with a conditional formatting rule to highlight any changed cells and then someone goes through and compiles all the edited lines + checks contributors to create a csv to check into BIAV github
the key task of a project coordinator here would be to lower the technological overhead as much as possible, so whatever the change is, I think it should be done in a way that maximizes accessibility

Thing is, most people here that have experience working as translators (volunteers or professional) are used to super cool tools that completely mask the nature of the data and present them with a tidy interface and next to no organisational work.

I'd be down to try and move towards that if possible, since I'm pretty sure that would be better because of how much friction would be removed.
meeeeeee, god I'd really like some actual proper tools but Ace said they don't have the resources for it
one day, maybe
please....

lol my writeup is taking forever and hit the character limit and I have more to write

Ideally, we could discuss with the studio what they could provide us with at a very low cost for them (i.e. what can they do in <0.5 days of dev time) to help.
For example changing to an organized format that splits the source files thematically and maybe even by language (so that people working on a language have a net 0% chance of messing things up elsewhere) would mean it could be trivial to parse it, and then maybe I could just try to make a slightly more human interface for it
agreed, but we have zero dev communication available to us currently which is kind of unfortunate
[Sadge](https://cdn.discordapp.com/emojis/793117136725344266.webp?size=48&name=Sadge)

The other thing is, I'd be down to tackle that conversion if it meant people could find their way around files more easily, but then that means I'd need to force that onto everyone, which is … ass ?
If we're changing the way people contribute, we should be doing that at most 1 time ever and with the explicit support of the studio + moving to a method that is absolutely 100% clearer & easier for everyone, not just what I think would suit them, which is why I've been reluctant to push hard for a change & observing how people are using the current system.

(and which is why I've come to the conclusion that right now, more knowledge seems to be a better solution to the friction rather than a change to what Mercy proposed)

-# I was too busy writing this to actually read and be responding to any messages past my "I work on weekdays" comment, so...
Okay so as for realistic application of systems, I'll give some examples that I'm struggling to understand how Git would handle without the way I'm organizing things. Say for the sake of argument, all files in the repo are UTF-8 .csv. **Correct me if I'm wrong about any part of how each scenario functions within Git.**

- Someone downloads the file for Ogier's Psyche Deepdive, "The Knight's Wish.csv", so that they can do the RU translation for it. It contains every column present in the source files, with all of the data that comes with it.
- They get about 1/3 of the way through, and unbeknownst to them, someone different downloads the same file to start working on the ID translation.
- The ID volunteer finishes their work, uploads the file "The Knight's Wish.csv" with all of the data in every column but ID exactly the same as they started with.
- Git shows the changes they made by highlighting in green the entire rows that contain any cell that they changed the ID column for. They commit the change, and the base file is updated accordingly. 
- The RU volunteer finishes their work, uploads the file "The Knight's Wish.csv" with all of the data in every column but RU exactly the same as they started with.
- Git shows the changes they made by highlighting in green the entire rows that contain any cell that they changed the RU column for, and highlights in red the entire rows in which the RU column did not change, but that the ID column had been changed for by the ID volunteer.

- The RU volunteer now has many choices.
  - Scenario #1: The RU volunteer makes the choice to commit the changes, overwriting all of the ID volunteer's work. This kills the crab.
  - Scenario #2: The RU volunteer sees the lines in red, and clears, but does not delete, those rows from their sheet making sure that only the rows that contain the changes they made are committed, overwriting the part of the ID volunteer's work that overlaps. This kills the crab.
  - Scenario #3: The RU volunteer pours over the insanely long lines to try to figure out what's wrong, and correctly concludes the ID column was changed, so they delete the ID column from the worksheet entirely, then finds that Git highlights in red everything but what they changed, as *every row* has been changed, because the ID column would be filled with VI. This kills the crab.
  - Scenario #4a: The RU volunteer clears, but doesn't delete, every column except RU. They upload the file, commit, and the base file now has no data except the RU column. This kills the crab.
  - Scenario #4b: I'm wrong about how blank columns work, so the RU volunteer clears, but doesn't delete, every column except RU. They upload the file, commit, and the base file *only* changes the RU column. Success!
  - Scenario #5: The RU volunteer uploads their file under a different name. We're back to square 1.
  - Scenario #6: The RU volunteer comes here to look for a guide, or ask for help on how Git works. We're back to square 1.
In my opinion, returning to square 1 kills the crab.

There are plenty more scenarios I could build, but that would take even longer to write.

Alright so, there's still a layer of having to have your changes accepted that you seems to have missed.

- scenario #1 : Ace/BIAV sees the RU changes coming in, git shows them that accepting those changes would wipe the recently added ID work, Ace/BIAV refuses and informs the RU people they need to correctly fuse their lines to make sure they're only pushing their work on top of the ID one, not in place of.
- scenario #2 : same
- scenario #3 : same
- scenario #4a : same
- scenario #4b : same (no it doesn't work like that, what you see is what you send, you're not editing the difference between states, you're editing states/revisions of a file)
- scenario #5 : impossible in a unique source scenario, only makes sens in the current situation (which doesn't make sense so that's the problem)

Basically, you're highlighting that having multilingual files suck.
I agree with that idea.
However, this is not a downgrade of the current system by any means.
I'll try and make an example to show it

Example :
the EN group has submitted their (substantial) changes, and BIAV accepts.
This is what's shown to whoever is accepting the changes

Now the FR group also has modification pending approval, but since they did it before the EN changes were accepted, their version doesn't have it, this means **conflict**.
This is what's shown to Ace/BIAV :

This is what conflict resolution looks like on github itself

You get the two versions of the line side-by side with the raw git markers and then ~~only one can survive~~ you mash them together or kill one as you see fit.
Now if you kill the master with the EN changes, it's gonna show up, and Ace/BIAV will just reject your changes since they brign regression ,and you can just do this agin without removing other people's work this time

I understand it's very late for you, so I won't fault you for not coming up with a more functional solution to the problem at hand yet. I'm just trying to respond to the argument you've presented, which if I'm not mistaken, was:
> Your complicated repo proposal is more effort than it's worth, because Git itself can handle the problem on its own if implemented and utilized correctly.

What I'm reading here is that, with the current situation we're in (having multilingual files, and an organizational structure reminiscent of chickens with their heads cut off), using Git alone will not actually help, as either (1) people would need to take significant extra steps and read potentially complicated guides to be able to not mess something up, or (2) "Ace/BIAV" would have to do extra work to make sure everything fits neatly together *(which is literally the proposed problem at hand.)*

The problems I'm trying to solve are as follows:
1. Not everyone here knows exactly how the current system works, and we need a user-friendly, simplistic system that **offloads any potentially complicated work onto an individual who knows how to not mess it up.**
2. Currently, said individual is Ace/BIAV, and that's not working as well as we'd like, so **I'm stepping up to the plate to be that individual.**
3. We don't have access to fancy tools, so we have to make do with what we have.
4. The "barrier of entry" for volunteers to correctly submit their work needs to be as low as possible, and any guides and instructions need to be as short and understandable as possible.

In the mean time, I'm going to continue forward with the solution I've proposed that materially solves the problems of (1) Ace/BIAV needs to have to do less work on behalf of the project, and (2) volunteers need to have to do less work to contribute to the project.
